name: Business Benefits of Cloud Computing
description: Core business value drivers that cloud computing provides over on-premises infrastructure, including agility, cost savings, and innovation speed.
key_facts: Cloud enables OpEx model instead of CapEx, shifting from large upfront investments to pay-as-you-go
Scalability allows businesses to grow or shrink resources on demand without hardware procurement delays
Global reach enables companies to serve customers worldwide from Google's 37+ regions
Reliability is backed by Google's global infrastructure with 99.99%+ SLA for many services
Speed of innovation increases as teams can provision environments in minutes rather than months
common_misconceptions: Cloud is always cheaper than on-premises — total cost depends on workload patterns and proper optimization
Moving to cloud automatically makes applications more secure — security is a shared responsibility

name: Shared Responsibility Model
description: The division of security duties between Google Cloud and the customer, defining what Google manages versus what the customer must secure.
key_facts: Google is responsible for security OF the cloud: physical security, hardware, and the global network
Customers are responsible for security IN the cloud: data, IAM, application configuration
Responsibility shift varies by service type: IaaS gives customers more control and responsibility than SaaS
Cloud Run and App Engine (PaaS) means Google manages OS patching; Compute Engine (IaaS) customers must patch
Data classification and protection is always the customer's responsibility regardless of service type
common_misconceptions: Google handles all security concerns when you use Google Cloud — customers still own data security and IAM
SaaS means zero security responsibility — customers still control access, data governance, and compliance

name: Google Cloud IAM Overview
description: Identity and Access Management (IAM) controls who can do what on which Google Cloud resources using a unified permissions system.
key_facts: IAM uses the principle of least privilege: grant only the permissions needed for a task
Three main IAM principals: Google Accounts, Service Accounts, and Google Groups
Roles bundle permissions: Basic roles (Owner/Editor/Viewer), Predefined roles, and Custom roles
IAM policies are attached at organization, folder, project, or resource level and inherit downward
Service accounts are non-human identities used by applications and VMs to authenticate to GCP APIs
common_misconceptions: Editor role is safe to give developers — it grants broad write access including potentially destructive operations
IAM policies at the project level don't affect resources within that project — they do inherit

name: Types of Cloud Data and Storage
description: Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data types and the Google Cloud storage services best suited to each.
key_facts: Structured data (rows/columns) is best served by relational databases like Cloud SQL or Cloud Spanner
Unstructured data (images, videos, documents) is stored in Cloud Storage as objects
Semi-structured data (JSON, XML) works well in Firestore or Bigtable
BigQuery is a managed data warehouse optimized for analytical (OLAP) queries on massive datasets
Hot data (frequently accessed) costs more than cold/archive data; Cloud Storage has multiple tiers
common_misconceptions: Cloud Storage is a file system like a hard drive — it is object storage accessed via HTTP APIs, not a POSIX filesystem
BigQuery replaces operational databases — it is analytical only and not suited for OLTP transactional workloads

name: BigQuery for Business Analytics
description: BigQuery is Google's fully managed, serverless data warehouse that enables super-fast SQL queries using the processing power of Google's infrastructure.
key_facts: BigQuery separates storage and compute, allowing independent scaling of each
Serverless: no infrastructure to manage; Google handles provisioning and scaling automatically
Supports standard SQL and integrates with Data Studio, Looker, and third-party BI tools
BigQuery ML allows training and running ML models directly with SQL without exporting data
On-demand pricing charges per TB of data scanned; flat-rate pricing offers reserved slots for predictable cost
common_misconceptions: BigQuery is slow for real-time queries — it is optimized for large analytical queries but has streaming inserts for near-real-time
You need a DBA to manage BigQuery — it is fully serverless with no index tuning or schema partitioning required

name: Google Cloud Compute Options
description: The spectrum of compute services from IaaS (Compute Engine) through containers (GKE) to serverless (Cloud Functions), each offering different levels of control and management.
key_facts: Compute Engine is IaaS providing virtual machines with full OS control and customizable machine types
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) manages containerized workloads orchestrated by Kubernetes
App Engine is a PaaS that automatically scales web apps without managing servers
Cloud Run is a serverless container platform that scales to zero and charges only when handling requests
Cloud Functions is event-driven serverless compute for single-purpose functions triggered by events
common_misconceptions: Serverless means no servers exist — servers exist but are fully managed and abstracted away by Google
Containers require Kubernetes — Cloud Run runs containers without Kubernetes cluster management overhead

name: Application Modernization Strategies
description: The 6 Rs of cloud migration (Rehost, Replatform, Repurchase, Refactor, Retire, Retain) and how to choose the right approach for each workload.
key_facts: Lift-and-shift (Rehost) moves workloads to cloud VMs with minimal changes, fastest migration path
Replatform makes minor optimizations (e.g., managed DB) without changing core architecture
Refactor/Re-architect redesigns applications as cloud-native microservices for maximum cloud benefit
Repurchase replaces a custom app with a SaaS solution (e.g., replacing on-prem CRM with Salesforce)
Retire identifies workloads that are no longer needed and can be decommissioned
common_misconceptions: Lift-and-shift achieves full cloud benefits — it is only the first step; optimization requires replatforming or refactoring
Refactoring is always the best choice — it has the highest cost and risk and is only justified for strategic workloads

name: Google Cloud AI and ML Products Overview
description: Google Cloud's layered AI/ML stack from pre-trained APIs to custom model training on Vertex AI, enabling organizations at every ML maturity level.
key_facts: Pre-trained AI APIs (Vision, Speech, Natural Language, Translation) require no ML expertise to use
AutoML enables training custom models on your own data without writing ML code
Vertex AI is the unified ML platform for building, training, and deploying custom models at scale
BigQuery ML lets data analysts train ML models using SQL directly on their data warehouse
Google's AI Principles guide responsible development: fairness, interpretability, privacy, and security
common_misconceptions: You need ML expertise to use Google Cloud AI — pre-trained APIs and AutoML require no ML knowledge
AI and ML are the same thing — ML is a subset of AI focused on learning from data patterns

name: Google Cloud Trust and Security Fundamentals
description: How Google Cloud protects customer data through encryption, compliance certifications, and transparent security practices built into its global infrastructure.
key_facts: All data at rest in Google Cloud is encrypted by default using AES-256 encryption
Data in transit is encrypted using TLS, and Google also encrypts traffic between its own data centers
Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) with Cloud KMS give customers control over key lifecycle
Google Cloud holds compliance certifications including ISO 27001, SOC 2, PCI DSS, and HIPAA
Transparency Reports and Cloud Trust Principles document Google's data access and government request policies
common_misconceptions: You must enable encryption manually — encryption at rest and in transit is on by default for all GCP services
Google can read your data whenever it wants — strict access controls and audit logs govern all internal access

name: Google's Global Network Infrastructure
description: Google Cloud's privately owned global network of subsea cables, points of presence, and regions that provides low latency and high reliability worldwide.
key_facts: Google operates one of the world's largest private networks spanning over 100,000 miles of subsea cable
Google Cloud has 37+ regions and 110+ zones globally as of 2024
Regions are independent geographic areas; zones are isolated deployment areas within a region
Premium Network Tier routes traffic over Google's private network for lowest latency; Standard Tier uses public internet
Edge nodes (Points of Presence) cache content close to users to reduce latency via Cloud CDN
common_misconceptions: More regions always means higher cost — running in a single region is often sufficient and cheaper for many workloads
Zones within a region are completely isolated from each other — they share the same regional infrastructure but have separate power/cooling

name: Sustainability and Google Cloud
description: Google Cloud's commitment to carbon neutrality, renewable energy purchasing, and how migrating to Google Cloud helps organizations meet their own sustainability goals.
key_facts: Google has been carbon neutral since 2007 and runs on 100% renewable energy on an annual basis
Google Cloud aims to run on carbon-free energy 24/7 by 2030 in every region
Carbon Footprint tool lets customers measure and report their GCP-related emissions
Data center PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) for Google is approximately 1.1 vs industry average of 1.58
Migrating to Google Cloud can significantly reduce an organization's carbon footprint compared to on-premises data centers
common_misconceptions: Using Google Cloud automatically makes your company carbon neutral — you still need to account for your own usage and supply chain
Renewable energy certificates equal 24/7 carbon-free energy — RECs are annual offsets, not real-time clean energy matching

name: Creating Value from Data
description: The data value chain from raw data collection through processing, analysis, and insight generation, and how Google Cloud services support each stage.
key_facts: The data lifecycle: Ingest -> Store -> Process -> Analyze -> Visualize -> Act
Pub/Sub handles real-time streaming ingestion; Cloud Storage handles batch data landing
Dataflow processes streaming and batch data using Apache Beam pipelines
Looker and Looker Studio (formerly Data Studio) are Google's BI tools for dashboards and reports
Data democratization means making data accessible to non-technical users through self-service BI tools
common_misconceptions: More data always means better insights — data quality and relevance matters more than volume
Business intelligence and AI are the same — BI analyzes past data while AI/ML predicts future outcomes

name: Cloud Operations and Monitoring
description: Google Cloud's operations suite (formerly Stackdriver) for monitoring, logging, and alerting to maintain visibility and reliability of cloud workloads.
key_facts: Cloud Monitoring collects metrics from GCP services and custom applications via APIs or agents
Cloud Logging centralizes logs from all GCP services, virtual machines, and custom applications
Error Reporting automatically analyzes and aggregates errors from application logs
Cloud Trace identifies performance bottlenecks in distributed applications and microservices
Alerting policies trigger notifications via email, PagerDuty, or Pub/Sub when metrics breach thresholds
common_misconceptions: Cloud operations tools only work for GCP services — they also support hybrid and multi-cloud monitoring with agents
Logging is only useful when something goes wrong — proactive log analysis reveals performance trends and security anomalies

name: Digital Transformation with Cloud
description: How organizations use cloud to enable business agility, innovation velocity, and new digital business models through the three waves of digital transformation.
key_facts: Digital transformation involves changing how an organization operates and delivers value using technology
The three waves: digitization (analog to digital), digitalization (process improvement), digital transformation (new business models)
Cloud enables faster experimentation through disposable environments and on-demand resources
DevOps practices (CI/CD) accelerate software delivery from months to hours using GCP tools
Google's DORA research identifies four key DevOps metrics: deployment frequency, lead time, MTTR, and change failure rate
common_misconceptions: Digital transformation is primarily a technology project — it is fundamentally a people and culture change enabled by technology
Cloud migration equals digital transformation — migration is infrastructure modernization; transformation requires rethinking processes

name: Generative AI on Google Cloud
description: Large language models, generative AI capabilities, and how Google Cloud's Gemini models and Vertex AI enable organizations to build AI-powered applications.
key_facts: Generative AI creates new content (text, images, code, audio) based on patterns learned from training data
Gemini is Google's multimodal foundation model available via Vertex AI and Google AI Studio
Vertex AI Model Garden provides access to Google, open-source, and third-party foundation models
Grounding connects generative AI outputs to factual data sources to reduce hallucinations
Responsible AI practices for generative AI include human oversight, bias testing, and output transparency
common_misconceptions: Generative AI always produces accurate information — LLMs can hallucinate plausible-sounding but incorrect facts
You need to build your own LLM — most organizations use pre-trained foundation models via APIs with fine-tuning or RAG
